text: Does the coupled Burgers-swept-KdV system decompose a Gaussian wave packet v(x,0)=4*exp(-(x+5)^2/2.25), u(x,0)=0 into a train of >=2 well-separated solitons (each amp>=0.8) by T=6.0, with mass(v) drift < 8%?

method: Fourier pseudospectral derivatives (all terms) + explicit RK4 in time, NO dealiasing, NO splitting, NO filter
bank_use_rationale: Progressive-complexity discipline mandates simplest baseline as E1 to localize failure mode. Choosing Fourier spectral derivatives over central FD because periodic domain and smooth Gaussian IC make spectral natural; central FD on the Burgers advection term is universally bad per kb-general-centralFD-hyperbolic-shockFormation. Choosing explicit RK4 (textbook) over IMEX/splitting per discipline. Negative entries rejected: kb-kdv-explicit-RK4-stiffness-blowup and kb-gardner-G1-explicitRK4-finiteFrag both warn explicit treatment of v_xxx fragments at modest amps — at amp 4 this will likely be worse, but I run it to confirm and learn the failure mode (will it NaN, or fragment with finite output?). dt=2e-5 chosen ~ dx^3 where dx=30/256 ~0.117 so dx^3~1.6e-3 / safety~80; explicit RK4 stability for v_xxx needs |k^3| dt < 2.83/|k_max|^3 ~ 2.83/(pi*256/30)^3 ~ 2.83/19500 ~1.45e-4, so dt=2e-5 is safe by ~7x. Will see if amp=4 nonlinear CFL also holds: max|6v+1.5v^2|~48, k_max~26.8, NL-CFL=2e-5*48*26.8~0.026 — should be fine.

diagnostics.overflow_loc: u*v and v*v in nonlinear term

rationale: Pure explicit RK4 + spectral (no dealiasing) at amp=4 blows up to NaN at t=0.388, well before T=6.0. Overflow originates in nonlinear products u*v and v*v during the explicit nonlinear step. Linear v_xxx stability dt-bound (dt<1.45e-4) was respected at dt=2e-5, so blow-up is driven by (a) aliasing of cubic-like products v*v_x amplifying high-k modes (consistent with kb-kdv-noDealiasing-aliasing-artifacts), and (b) amplitude-CFL on the explicit nonlinear part: at amp=4, max|6v+1.5v^2|=24+24=48 vs amp=1.5 baseline ~12.4, so the nonlinear CFL is 4x tighter — even though the bare CFL estimate looked safe, aliasing-fed instability dominates first. Finding pins the bottleneck: it is the nonlinear term, not the linear dispersive term. Single-component fix is dealiasing (positive entries unavailable but kb-kdv-noDealiasing-aliasing-artifacts and kb-gardner-G3-noDealiasing-cubicAliasing point directly to 2/3-rule dealiasing as the natural escalation).

Add 2/3-rule dealiasing as the single-component method upgrade — F1 pins the failure to aliasing-driven amplification of nonlinear products. Do NOT yet switch to IMEX-CN or splitting (per discipline: one component at a time). Keep dt=2e-5 unchanged: with dealiasing the effective k_max is N/3*2pi/L=17.87, so nonlinear CFL = dt*48*17.87 = 0.017 (safe) and v_xxx linear stability = dt*17.87^3 = 0.114 << 2.83 (safe). The simplest hypothesis is: dealiasing alone fixes E1.

method: Fourier pseudospectral derivatives + explicit RK4 + 2/3-rule dealiasing on nonlinear products (single-component upgrade vs E1)
bank_use_rationale: Single component changed from E1: added 2/3-rule dealiasing — directly addresses F1's diagnosis (nonlinear product overflow driven by aliasing). kb-kdv-noDealiasing-aliasing-artifacts and kb-gardner-G3-noDealiasing-cubicAliasing both confirm: spectral methods without dealiasing produce spurious peaks and amplitude inflation; the cubic term v^2*v_x aliases up to 3x Nyquist. Did NOT also switch time integrator (would be 2-component change). dt kept at 2e-5 to isolate the dealiasing effect: with dealiasing reducing the effective k_max to N/3*2pi/L=17.87, nonlinear CFL at amp=4 is 2e-5*48*17.87=0.017 (safe), v_xxx RK4 stability is 2e-5*17.87^3=0.114 (safe by factor 25). If E2 succeeds → dealiasing alone was the missing piece. If it still fragments/blows up → escalate to IMEX-CN (explicit RK4 on v_xxx may be marginal even with dealiasing).

rationale: E2 (explicit RK4 + Fourier spectral + 2/3 dealiasing) at amp=4 produces a stable run to T=6.0 with EXACTLY conserved mass (drift 0.000%, far below the 8% threshold) and 10 well-separated peaks above 0.8, well surpassing the >=2-peak phenomenon target. The peak heights span 0.85-1.70 — consistent with inverse-scattering decomposition of a wide Gaussian into multiple solitons of varying amplitudes; the broadest IC component (sigma=1.5, amp=4) carries enough action to spawn many soliton modes per integrable scattering. Min peak spacing 1.52 (~13 dx) is well-resolved. The coupling between u and v induces realistic mixed solitary structure with v developing negative lobes (~-1.5) and u reaching ~3.6 — consistent with the swept-KdV / Gardner-coupling dynamics described in the PDE. The single-component upgrade from E1 (just add 2/3 dealiasing) was sufficient to (a) eliminate aliasing-driven nonlinear overflow and (b) yield physically meaningful soliton decomposition. Mass conservation to machine precision suggests no leakage; no NaN at any snapshot. Per kb-general-massConservation-insufficient-diagnostic, mass alone isn't sufficient — but peak count + amplitude + position together pass the phenomenon target with comfortable margin.

E2 meets the phenomenon target with margin: mass drift 0% < 8%, 10 peaks (>=2 required) each with amplitude >=0.85 (>=0.8 required), well-separated by ~1.52. Stop early per protocol; the simplest viable method (explicit RK4 + spectral + 2/3 dealiasing) is the final answer. Avoiding E3 escalation prevents over-engineering, which negative entries kb-kdv-explicit-RK4-stiffness-blowup and kb-kdv-IFRK4-blowup warn carries its own risks.
